data.title: Audinate Group H1 2026 Loss Widens To A$0.05 EPS And Tests Bullish Software Narrative
data.description:
Advertisement

Audinate Group (ASX:AD8) H1 2026 earnings snapshot

Audinate Group (ASX:AD8) has reported its H1 2026 numbers with total revenue of A$33.3 million and a basic EPS loss of A$0.05, while trailing twelve month figures show revenue of A$65.5 million and a basic EPS loss of A$0.18. The company has seen revenue move from A$44.9 million in H2 2024 to A$28.7 million in H1 2025 and A$33.3 million in H2 2025, alongside a shift from basic EPS of A$0.07 to losses of A$0.03 and A$0.05. This sets up a results season in which investors may weigh top line progress against ongoing earnings pressure and compressed margins.

See our full analysis for Audinate Group.

With the headline figures on the table, the next step is to see how these earnings line up with the widely followed growth and profitability narratives that have built up around Audinate and how those stories may be challenged by the latest numbers.

See what the community is saying about Audinate Group

Losses widen on trailing basis

  • On a trailing twelve month view, Audinate reported A$65.5 million in revenue and a net loss of A$14.8 million, compared with a net loss of A$6.4 million in the prior trailing period.
  • Consensus narrative expects mid teens annual revenue growth of about 15.6%, yet the current trailing loss contrasts with that, as:
    • Audinate moved from a profit of A$5.5 million in H2 2024 to losses of A$2.2 million and A$4.2 million in H1 and H2 2025, which sits against the growth story built around expanding Dante adoption and new products.
    • Bulls point to longer term loss reduction of roughly 41.4% per year over five years, but the latest A$14.8 million trailing loss shows that turning that trend into consistent profitability is still an open task.

Revenue growth vs rising cost base

  • Half year revenue has shifted from A$44.9 million in H2 2024 to A$28.7 million in H1 2025 and A$33.3 million in H2 2025, while net income moved from a A$5.5 million profit to losses of A$2.2 million and A$4.2 million over the same periods.
  • Bulls argue that shifting toward higher margin software and SaaS should eventually help margins, yet current figures highlight a trade off, as:
    • Management is expecting operating costs to rise by about 25% in FY 2026 as products like Dante Director and Iris are scaled, which sits alongside the recent move into loss for H2 2025.
    • Supporters see this spend as laying groundwork for recurring SaaS revenue, but the combination of a A$4.2 million loss in H2 2025 and the larger A$14.8 million trailing loss means the path from investment to earnings is still being proven in the reported numbers.
Have bulls got this shift to software and cloud right, or are the recent losses a warning sign to take more seriously for now? 🐂 Audinate Group Bull Case

Mixed signals on valuation and profitability

  • With the share price at A$3.30 and a P/S of 4.5x, Audinate trades below the cited peer average of 9.4x but above the Global Electronic industry average of 1.9x, while analysts’ quoted DCF fair value sits at A$17.56.
  • Bears question whether current profitability justifies even this P/S level, and the reported results give them some backing, as:
    • The company is unprofitable on a trailing basis and is not forecast to be profitable over the next three years, which lines up with the recent shift from a A$5.5 million profit in H2 2024 to losses through 2025.
    • Forecast revenue growth of about 14.9% per year comes alongside expectations that losses will continue, so skeptics focus on the risk that higher operating costs and competitive pressure could keep profits under strain even if revenue rises.
If you are weighing whether the current A$3.30 price already reflects these risks, it can help to see how cautious investors frame the same numbers. 🐻 Audinate Group Bear Case

Approach 1: Alvotech Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes estimates of a company’s future cash flows and discounts them back to today to arrive at an estimate of what the business might be worth per share.

For Alvotech, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is a loss of $134.54 million, so the story here is based on what future cash flows could look like rather than current profitability. Analysts provide early year estimates, and Simply Wall St then extrapolates further out. For example, free cash flow for 2026 is projected at $51.00 million, rising to $682.93 million by 2035, with each of these future figures discounted back to today.

Putting all those projected cash flows together, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about US$28.80 per share. Compared with the current share price of US$4.64, this implies the stock is 83.9% undervalued according to this DCF output.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: Alvotech Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is often a useful shorthand for what the market is willing to pay today for each dollar of earnings. It lets you quickly compare how the market is pricing businesses that already generate earnings, without getting into more complex cash flow models.

What counts as a "normal" or "fair" P/E really depends on what investors expect from the company. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk tends to line up with a lower P/E.

Alvotech currently trades on a P/E of 20.90x. That sits below the Biotechs industry average of 22.20x and also below the peer group average of 43.68x. Simply Wall St also calculates a proprietary "Fair Ratio" of 26.59x, which is an estimate of what Alvotech's P/E might be given factors such as its earnings profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and risk characteristics.

This Fair Ratio is more tailored than a simple peer or industry comparison, because it adjusts for company specific features rather than assuming all Biotech names deserve the same multiple. Setting the current 20.90x P/E against the 26.59x Fair Ratio suggests the shares are trading below that Fair Ratio estimate.

data.title: Bendigo And Adelaide Bank (ASX:BEN) Loan Growth Story Tested By Trailing Earnings Losses
data.description:
Bendigo and Adelaide Bank (ASX:BEN) has put a mixed set of numbers on the table for H1 2026, with trailing twelve month revenue at A$1.98b and basic EPS at a loss of A$0.15 per share, alongside trailing net income excluding extra items of A$83.3 million in losses. Over recent periods the bank has seen revenue hold around the A$1.9b mark on a trailing basis, while basic EPS has shifted from A$0.85 per share in H1 2025 to a loss of A$0.17 per share by H2 2025, before landing at the current trailing loss of A$0.15. This keeps the focus on how margins can stabilise and potentially rebuild.

See our full analysis for Bendigo and Adelaide Bank.

With the headline figures in place, the next step is to set these results against the widely followed narratives around Bendigo and Adelaide Bank's growth, risks and profitability to see which stories hold up and which start to look out of date.

See what the community is saying about Bendigo and Adelaide Bank

Advertisement

Loan book grows above A$85b while profits slip

  • Total loans reached A$85,924.2 million in H2 2025, up from A$83,572.6 million in H1 2025 and A$80,801.1 million in H2 2024, even as net income excluding extra items moved from a profit of A$262.7 million in H2 2024 to a loss of A$313.9 million in H2 2025.
  • Analysts' consensus view discusses strong balance sheet growth and mortgage growth running at roughly twice system growth. The loan figures support that growth angle but also sit beside trailing 12 month net income losses of A$83.3 million, which means the growth favoured by bullish investors is currently paired with weaker profitability than the consensus narrative assumes.

Margins and costs feel the pressure

  • The reported net interest margin held at 1.88% in both H1 and H2 2025, while the cost to income ratio edged from 61.5% in H1 2025 to 62.1% in H2 2025, pointing to relatively stable spreads but slightly heavier operating costs against revenue of around A$950 million per half.
  • Consensus narrative highlights higher spend on technology and risk management and rising funding costs as headwinds for margins, and the flat 1.88% net interest margin alongside a cost to income ratio above 60% supports that cautious angle because
    • increased expenses and competition for deposits, as described, are consistent with margins not widening despite a loan book above A$85b,
    • and the move from a profit of A$216.8 million in H1 2025 to a loss of A$313.9 million in H2 2025 aligns with concerns that earnings can be squeezed even while revenue stays near A$950 million per half.

Valuation discounts versus loss making status

  • The shares trade around A$11.20 against a P/B of 0.9x and a DCF fair value of A$11.01, while the bank is loss making on a trailing 12 month basis with net income excluding extra items at a loss of A$83.3 million and a dividend yield of 5.63% that is not covered by those trailing earnings.
  • Critics note that the bank has been unprofitable over the past five years, and that backdrop challenges any bullish view that a below peer P/B and a small discount to DCF fair value alone make the shares look appealing because
    • the combination of ongoing losses and a dividend not covered by current earnings points to cash flows that are not yet aligned with the payout,
    • and the modest revenue growth outlook of about 4% per year, compared with 6% for the Australian market, means the valuation gap to peers is set against a slower growth profile than the broader market.

data.title: Should You Buy Apollo Global Management, Inc. (NYSE:APO) For Its Upcoming Dividend?
data.description:
Apollo Global Management, Inc. (NYSE:APO) is about to trade ex-dividend in the next 2 days. The ex-dividend date is one business day before the record date, which is the cut-off date for shareholders to be present on the company's books to be eligible for a dividend payment. The ex-dividend date is important as the process of settlement involves a full business day. So if you miss that date, you would not show up on the company's books on the record date. Accordingly, Apollo Global Management investors that purchase the stock on or after the 19th of February will not receive the dividend, which will be paid on the 27th of February.

The company's next dividend payment will be US$0.51 per share, on the back of last year when the company paid a total of US$2.04 to shareholders. Last year's total dividend payments show that Apollo Global Management has a trailing yield of 1.6% on the current share price of US$125.07. We love seeing companies pay a dividend, but it's also important to be sure that laying the golden eggs isn't going to kill our golden goose! So we need to check whether the dividend payments are covered, and if earnings are growing.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Dividends are usually paid out of company profits, so if a company pays out more than it earned then its dividend is usually at greater risk of being cut. Fortunately Apollo Global Management's payout ratio is modest, at just 35% of profit.

Generally speaking, the lower a company's payout ratios, the more resilient its dividend usually is.

See our latest analysis for Apollo Global Management

Click here to see the company's payout ratio, plus analyst estimates of its future dividends.

Advertisement

Have Earnings And Dividends Been Growing?

Stocks in companies that generate sustainable earnings growth often make the best dividend prospects, as it is easier to lift the dividend when earnings are rising. If business enters a downturn and the dividend is cut, the company could see its value fall precipitously. That's why it's comforting to see Apollo Global Management's earnings have been skyrocketing, up 68% per annum for the past five years.

The main way most investors will assess a company's dividend prospects is by checking the historical rate of dividend growth. Apollo Global Management's dividend payments per share have declined at 3.4% per year on average over the past 10 years, which is uninspiring. It's unusual to see earnings per share increasing at the same time as dividends per share have been in decline. We'd hope it's because the company is reinvesting heavily in its business, but it could also suggest business is lumpy.

The Bottom Line

Is Apollo Global Management worth buying for its dividend? When companies are growing rapidly and retaining a majority of the profits within the business, it's usually a sign that reinvesting earnings creates more value than paying dividends to shareholders. Perhaps even more importantly - this can sometimes signal management is focused on the long term future of the business. In summary, Apollo Global Management appears to have some promise as a dividend stock, and we'd suggest taking a closer look at it.

In light of that, while Apollo Global Management has an appealing dividend, it's worth knowing the risks involved with this stock. For example, we've found 3 warning signs for Apollo Global Management that we recommend you consider before investing in the business.

data.title: INFRONEER Holdings (TSE:5076) Is Paying Out A Larger Dividend Than Last Year
data.description:
INFRONEER Holdings Inc.'s (TSE:5076) dividend will be increasing from last year's payment of the same period to ¥62.00 on 8th of June. This will take the dividend yield to an attractive 4.8%, providing a nice boost to shareholder returns.

While the dividend yield is important for income investors, it is also important to consider any large share price moves, as this will generally outweigh any gains from distributions. Investors will be pleased to see that INFRONEER Holdings' stock price has increased by 39% in the last 3 months, which is good for shareholders and can also explain a decrease in the dividend yield.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Advertisement

INFRONEER Holdings' Projected Earnings Seem Likely To Cover Future Distributions

While it is great to have a strong dividend yield, we should also consider whether the payment is sustainable. Before making this announcement, INFRONEER Holdings was paying a whopping 110% as a dividend, but this only made up 24% of its overall earnings. The business might be trying to strike a balance between returning cash to shareholders and reinvesting back into the business, but this high of a payout ratio could definitely force the dividend to be cut if the company runs into a bit of a tough spot.

EPS is set to fall by 0.8% over the next 12 months. Assuming the dividend continues along recent trends, we believe the payout ratio could be 42%, which we are pretty comfortable with and we think is feasible on an earnings basis.

See our latest analysis for INFRONEER Holdings

INFRONEER Holdings Is Still Building Its Track Record

The dividend has been pretty stable looking back, but the company hasn't been paying one for very long. This makes it tough to judge how it would fare through a full economic cycle. Since 2022, the annual payment back then was ¥10.00, compared to the most recent full-year payment of ¥124.00. This works out to be a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of approximately 88% a year over that time. It is always nice to see strong dividend growth, but with such a short payment history we wouldn't be inclined to rely on it until a longer track record can be developed.

The Dividend Looks Likely To Grow

Investors could be attracted to the stock based on the quality of its payment history. We are encouraged to see that INFRONEER Holdings has grown earnings per share at 21% per year over the past five years. Earnings have been growing rapidly, and with a low payout ratio we think that the company could turn out to be a great dividend stock.

Our Thoughts On INFRONEER Holdings' Dividend

In summary, while it's always good to see the dividend being raised, we don't think INFRONEER Holdings' payments are rock solid. While the low payout ratio is a redeeming feature, this is offset by the minimal cash to cover the payments. We would be a touch cautious of relying on this stock primarily for the dividend income.

Market movements attest to how highly valued a consistent dividend policy is compared to one which is more unpredictable. However, there are other things to consider for investors when analysing stock performance. To that end, INFRONEER Holdings has 2 warning signs (and 1 which makes us a bit uncomfortable) we think you should know about. Looking for more high-yielding dividend ideas? In light of this, it's understandable that Redsun Services Group's P/S would sit below the majority of other companies. However, we think shrinking revenues are unlikely to lead to a stable P/S over the longer term, which could set up shareholders for future disappointment. Even just maintaining these prices could be difficult to achieve as recent revenue trends are already weighing down the shares.

What We Can Learn From Redsun Services Group's P/S?

We'd say the price-to-sales ratio's power isn't primarily as a valuation instrument but rather to gauge current investor sentiment and future expectations.

Our examination of Redsun Services Group confirms that the company's shrinking revenue over the past medium-term is a key factor in its low price-to-sales ratio, given the industry is projected to grow. At this stage investors feel the potential for an improvement in revenue isn't great enough to justify a higher P/S ratio. Unless the recent medium-term conditions improve, they will continue to form a barrier for the share price around these levels.

data.title: We Think Monstarlab (TSE:5255) Is Taking Some Risk With Its Debt
data.description:
David Iben put it well when he said, 'Volatility is not a risk we care about. What we care about is avoiding the permanent loss of capital.' So it seems the smart money knows that debt - which is usually involved in bankruptcies - is a very important factor, when you assess how risky a company is. Importantly, Monstarlab Inc. (TSE:5255) does carry debt. But the real question is whether this debt is making the company risky.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Advertisement

When Is Debt A Problem?

Debt and other liabilities become risky for a business when it cannot easily fulfill those obligations, either with free cash flow or by raising capital at an attractive price. In the worst case scenario, a company can go bankrupt if it cannot pay its creditors. However, a more usual (but still expensive) situation is where a company must dilute shareholders at a cheap share price simply to get debt under control. Of course, the upside of debt is that it often represents cheap capital, especially when it replaces dilution in a company with the ability to reinvest at high rates of return. The first thing to do when considering how much debt a business uses is to look at its cash and debt together.

What Is Monstarlab's Debt?

The image below, which you can click on for greater detail, shows that Monstarlab had debt of JP¥5.88b at the end of September 2025, a reduction from JP¥9.49b over a year. However, it does have JP¥4.07b in cash offsetting this, leading to net debt of about JP¥1.81b.

A Look At Monstarlab's Liabilities

According to the last reported balance sheet, Monstarlab had liabilities of JP¥2.02b due within 12 months, and liabilities of JP¥6.44b due beyond 12 months. On the other hand, it had cash of JP¥4.07b and JP¥1.24b worth of receivables due within a year. So its liabilities outweigh the sum of its cash and (near-term) receivables by JP¥3.15b.

Monstarlab has a market capitalization of JP¥8.86b, so it could very likely raise cash to ameliorate its balance sheet, if the need arose. But it's clear that we should definitely closely examine whether it can manage its debt without dilution.

View our latest analysis for Monstarlab

We use two main ratios to inform us about debt levels relative to earnings. The first is net debt divided by earnings before interest, tax, depreciation, and amortization (EBITDA), while the second is how many times its earnings before interest and tax (EBIT) covers its interest expense (or its interest cover, for short). Thus we consider debt relative to earnings both with and without depreciation and amortization expenses.

With net debt sitting at just 0.57 times EBITDA, Monstarlab is arguably pretty conservatively geared. And it boasts interest cover of 7.1 times, which is more than adequate. Although Monstarlab made a loss at the EBIT level, last year, it was also good to see that it generated JP¥3.0b in EBIT over the last twelve months. The balance sheet is clearly the area to focus on when you are analysing debt. But it is Monstarlab's earnings that will influence how the balance sheet holds up in the future. So if you're keen to discover more about its earnings, it might be worth checking out this graph of its long term earnings trend.

But our final consideration is also important, because a company cannot pay debt with paper profits; it needs cold hard cash. So it's worth checking how much of the earnings before interest and tax (EBIT) is backed by free cash flow. Considering the last year, Monstarlab actually recorded a cash outflow, overall. Debt is far more risky for companies with unreliable free cash flow, so shareholders should be hoping that the past expenditure will produce free cash flow in the future.

Our View

Monstarlab's struggle to convert EBIT to free cash flow had us second guessing its balance sheet strength, but the other data-points we considered were relatively redeeming. For example its net debt to EBITDA was refreshing. Looking at all the angles mentioned above, it does seem to us that Monstarlab is a somewhat risky investment as a result of its debt. Not all risk is bad, as it can boost share price returns if it pays off, but this debt risk is worth keeping in mind. The balance sheet is clearly the area to focus on when you are analysing debt. However, not all investment risk resides within the balance sheet - far from it. For instance, we've identified 4 warning signs for Monstarlab (3 don't sit too well with us) you should be aware of.

Does Growth Match The High P/E?

There's an inherent assumption that a company should far outperform the market for P/E ratios like Hosoya Pyro-Engineering's to be considered reasonable.

Hosoya Pyro-Engineering's P/E is flying high just like its stock has during the last month. Over the last 12 months on a trailing basis, the company recorded total revenue of A$691.4 million and net income of A$16.9 million, with margins narrowing from earlier levels. Revenue has progressed from A$288.3 million in 1H FY 2024 to A$347.3 million in 2H FY 2024 and then to A$336.2 million in 1H FY 2025. EPS moved from A$0.01 in 1H FY 2024 to A$0.06 in 2H FY 2024 before this latest period’s loss, creating an earnings narrative that now depends on how quickly profitability can rebuild.

See our full analysis for oOh!media.

With the headline results disclosed, the next step is to assess how these margin trends compare with widely followed views on oOh!media’s earnings power and to identify where those narratives may need to be revised.

See what the community is saying about oOh!media

Advertisement

Margins Compress With 2.4% Net Profit

  • Over the last 12 months, oOh!media earned A$16.9 million on A$691.4 million of revenue, which works out to a 2.4% net profit margin compared with 5.8% a year earlier.
  • Bears focus on this margin squeeze, and the Auckland Transport contract loss in New Zealand feeds that concern:
    • Reported net margin falling to 2.4% and a one off A$30.0 million loss in the period both support the cautious view that profitability is under pressure.
    • Critics highlight that replacing lost high margin contracts may require 2x to 3x the revenue, so weaker margins could linger if new wins do not fully offset that mix shift.

🐻 oOh!media Bear Case

EPS Swings Around A$0.06 Peak

  • Basic EPS moved from A$0.01 in 1H FY 2024 to A$0.06 in 2H FY 2024, then to a loss of A$0.02 in 1H FY 2025, while trailing 12 month EPS was A$0.04 at the FY 2025 first half mark.
  • Consensus narrative talks about urban expansion and more digital assets supporting earnings growth, and the recent EPS path shows both support and friction for that bullish angle:
    • The move from A$288.3 million of revenue in 1H FY 2024 to A$347.3 million in 2H FY 2024 and A$336.2 million in 1H FY 2025 lines up with the idea that network reach is helping revenue, even if profit per dollar of sales has been uneven.
    • At the same time, the swing from A$30.7 million of net income in 2H FY 2024 to an A$11.3 million loss in 1H FY 2025 shows that higher revenue alone has not translated into steady bottom line progress yet.

🐂 oOh!media Bull Case

High P/E And DCF Gap Stand Out

  • The shares trade on a trailing P/E of 34.7x compared with a global media average of 16x, while a DCF fair value of A$2.08 sits well above the current A$1.09 share price.
  • Bulls argue that earnings growth can justify this mix of rich multiples and upside signals, and the current numbers frame that debate clearly:
    • Analysts in this dataset expect earnings to grow about 23.1% per year with revenue at roughly 4.4% per year, which is faster than the 12.1% earnings growth benchmark cited for the Australian market.
    • On the other hand, a 2.4% trailing net margin and a dividend yield of around 4.82% that is not well covered by earnings both show the business still has work to do to match the optimism implied by the P/E and DCF fair value gap.

data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (NYSE:TSM) After An 82% One Year Jump?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing's current share price still makes sense for new money, you are not alone. A closer look at what you are paying for is the focus of this article.
  • The stock recently closed at US$366.36, with returns of 3.1% over 7 days, 7.0% over 30 days, 14.6% year to date and 81.6% over 1 year, plus 321.8% over 3 years and 192.9% over 5 years.
  • These moves have come as Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing continues to sit at the center of long term themes around advanced chip manufacturing and global supply chains, with investors closely watching its position in the semiconductor industry. Broader attention on chipmakers has also kept the stock firmly on many watchlists. This gives important context for assessing whether the current price still looks reasonable.
  • On our simple valuation checklist, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing scores 3 out of 6. Next we will look at how different valuation methods line up with that score, then finish with a more complete way to think about value than any single metric alone.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing delivered 81.6% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Semiconductor industry.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model takes estimates of the cash a company may generate in the future, then discounts those amounts back to today to arrive at an estimate of what the business could be worth per share right now.

For Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing, the model used here is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is NT$900,193.91m, and analysts provide detailed projections for the next few years. Beyond that, Simply Wall St extrapolates cash flows out to 2035, with projected free cash flow in 2029 of NT$2,858,568.79m and a full set of discounted values for 10 years of forecasts.

When those projected cash flows are discounted back and summed, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of US$283.97 per share. Against the recent share price of US$366.36, this implies the stock trades at a 29.0% premium to the DCF estimate, which points to a rich valuation on this set of assumptions.

Result: OVERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing may be overvalued by 29.0%. Discover 54 high quality undervalued stocks or create your own screener to find better value opportunities.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing.

Approach 2: Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company, the P/E ratio is a useful way to think about what you are paying for each dollar of earnings. It gives a quick sense of how the market weighs the company’s earnings profile relative to others.

In general, higher growth expectations or lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while lower growth or higher risk usually point to a lower “normal” P/E range. Context is important when you look at any single multiple.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing currently trades on a P/E of 28.9x. That is below the Semiconductor industry average of 43.4x and the peer group average of 57.4x. On the surface, that suggests the stock is priced below many peers on an earnings basis.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing is 41.4x. This is a proprietary estimate of what a more appropriate P/E might be, based on factors like earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market cap and company specific risks. Because it blends these elements into a single figure, it can be more tailored than a simple comparison with industry or peer averages.

Comparing the Fair Ratio of 41.4x with the actual P/E of 28.9x indicates that the stock screens as undervalued on this metric.

Result: UNDERVALUED

P/E ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Start investing in legacies, not executives. Discover our 23 top founder-led companies.

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Narrative

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation, so let us introduce you to Narratives, which are simply your own story about a company, linked directly to a forecast for revenue, earnings and margins, and then to an assumed fair value that you can compare with today’s price.

On Simply Wall St’s Community page, Narratives let you set out your view in plain language, then connect it to numbers, so instead of only looking at a P/E or DCF, you can say why you think Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing might justify a fair value of US$55.12, US$118.40, US$400.00 or even US$798.09, and see how that compares with the recent price of US$366.36.

Because Narratives on the platform update as new information such as earnings, guidance or news is added, they provide a living framework to decide whether the gap between your Fair Value and the current Price suggests a time to add, trim or simply watch, based on your own assumptions rather than relying only on headline multiples.

For Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing, here are previews of two leading Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing narratives.

You can use these as starting points and then adjust the assumptions to match your own view on growth, margins and risk, rather than relying on a single fair value number.

🐂 Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Bull Case

Fair value in this bull case narrative: US$400.00 per share

Gap to that fair value versus the recent US$366.36 price: about 8.4% below the narrative fair value

Revenue growth assumption used in this narrative: 76.64%

  • Sees TSMC as the central infrastructure provider for AI and high performance computing, with a large share of global advanced chip production for customers such as Apple, Nvidia and other hyperscalers.
  • Highlights recent quarterly results that include high revenue, strong net income and margins, supported by demand for 3 nm and other advanced nodes.
  • Assumes continued expansion of fabs in Taiwan, the U.S., Japan and Europe, ongoing high margins and balance sheet strength, with geopolitical and concentration risks viewed as manageable.

🐻 Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Bear Case

Fair value in this more cautious narrative: US$118.40 per share

Gap to that fair value versus the recent US$366.36 price: about 209.3% above the narrative fair value

Revenue growth assumption used in this narrative: 23.21%

  • Focuses on long term risks around China Taiwan relations, exposure to a small number of key customers and reliance on a limited number of advanced equipment suppliers such as ASML.
  • Assumes revenue growth around 10% a year with a 40% net margin and uses a 20x future P/E to arrive at a fair value that is well below the recent share price.
  • Views the current price as rich relative to these assumptions, even though it acknowledges TSMC's strong balance sheet, long dividend record and role in the chip sector.

Together, these two narratives show how different assumptions on growth, margins, risk and required return can lead to very different views on fair value for the same stock, even when both start from the same publicly available data.

data.title: Schouw Shareholders Alter Bond Guarantees As BioMar Role Scales Back
data.description:

  • The decision removes BioMar from its status as a Material Subsidiary in the bond documentation.
  • The approval was granted by a majority vote of shareholders.

Aktieselskabet Schouw & Co. (CPSE:SCHO) is a Danish industrial group with holdings across several sectors, and BioMar has been one of its key subsidiaries. The shareholder-backed decision to remove BioMar as an Original Guarantor under existing bonds reshapes how credit risk is shared within the group. For you as an investor, this is primarily about how security is structured rather than day-to-day operations.

This change can matter if you hold, or are assessing, Schouw bonds, because the guarantee package tied to those instruments has been adjusted. It may also influence how you think about the link between the parent company and BioMar when assessing overall group risk, capital structure, and future financing choices.
